topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the kitchen counter like a ticking time bomb. Liam stared at the crisp white paper, his breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. "Moving?" he managed to croak out, the word sounding alien and brittle in the quiet apartment. He reread the address, the unfamiliar state name blurring slightly. A coldness, sharp and invasive, began to seep into his fingertips. He slowly lowered the paper, his hand trembling so violently he had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to steady himself. Every mundane sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, grating against the sudden, sickening realization that his anchor, his constant, Alex, was physically erasing himself from their shared reality. He squeezed his eyes shut, a tight knot forming beneath his ribs, and fought the desperate urge to crumple the letter into a useless ball.

[story 2]

"You're serious?" Maya’s voice was a thin, reedy sound that seemed to evaporate the warmth from the room. She didn't look up from the map spread across the coffee table, tracing a line with a hesitant finger. When she finally lifted her gaze, her eyes were wide, swimming with a frantic, unreadable panic. "I... I got the acceptance," she whispered, her voice cracking on the last word. He felt a physical lurch in his stomach, a sudden, nauseating drop. It wasn't just a change of scenery; it was the dismantling of their entire established constellation. He pushed himself up from the sofa, his movements stiff, jerky. "Where are you going?" His voice was dangerously low, stripped bare of its usual easy cadence. He couldn't meet her eyes; the sheer magnitude of the shift made his throat constrict, making it impossible to form a coherent question.

[story 3]

I found the news tucked inside a library book, the glossy paper feeling strangely heavy and unreal in my hands. The details swam before my eyes: a new city, a new life, a move across the continent. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, a tremor that started in my wrists and radiated outward, making it difficult to hold the book steady. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the familiar view of the street below. The air felt suddenly too thin, too sharp. Every memory of shared late-night talks, of inside jokes etched into our history, rushed in a tidal wave of painful clarity. I turned back, and the sight of the envelope seemed to pulse with an aggressive finality. I let out a sound—a ragged, involuntary exhale that sounded more like a sob trapped deep in my chest—and I couldn't stop pacing, my feet pounding an uneven rhythm on the hardwood floor.
